subtopic_detail: Bicarbonate threshold and indication in tricyclic antidepressant overdose with widened QRS
stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 22-year-old woman two hours after ingestion of an unknown quantity of amitriptyline tablets in a suicide attempt. She had a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure on arrival lasting one minute. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 130 bpm with QRS 140 ms and right axis deviation in lead aVR with terminal R wave 4 mm. She is now drowsy with shallow breathing.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 130, RR 10, BP 86/54, SpO2 92 percent, GCS 9
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, dry mucosa, mydriasis, hyperreflexia, no focal weakness
data_table.Investigations: ECG QRS 140 ms with terminal R wave in aVR 4 mm; VBG pH 7.30 lactate 3.8 mmol/L; bedside glucose 5.2
data_table.Medications: No regular medications; ingestion 2 hours ago

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Administer intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg bolus and titrate against conscious level for reversal
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in TCA overdose as it lowers seizure threshold further. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous adenosine 6 mg rapid push to terminate the wide complex tachycardia rhythm
options.rationale: This rhythm is sinus with sodium channel blockade, not a re-entrant SVT; adenosine has no role here. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg as a bolus to narrow the QRS interval
options.rationale: QRS over 100 ms with R wave in aVR over 3 mm indicates sodium channel blockade and is the threshold for IV sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: Administer intravenous glucagon 5 mg slow push for refractory hypotension and bradycardia presumed
options.rationale: Glucagon is for beta-blocker overdose; she is tachycardic with sodium channel blockade requiring bicarbonate. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube for late presentation decontamination first
options.rationale: Charcoal is contraindicated with reduced GCS and unprotected airway; secure airway and correct sodium channel blockade first. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes sodium channel blockade with QRS widening, terminal R wave in aVR, hypotension, and seizures. Treatment of QRS over 100 ms or R wave in aVR over 3 mm is intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus repeated until QRS narrows. Hypertonic saline is an alternative. Lidocaine is reserved for refractory ventricular arrhythmia. Activated charcoal is contraindicated with unprotected airway. Intralipid is a salvage option in cardiac arrest.
key_points: QRS over 100 ms or R in aVR over 3 mm = sodium channel blockade
Sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg IV bolus
Flumazenil contraindicated due to seizure risk
Secure airway before any oral charcoal
explanation.pearls: A terminal R wave in aVR greater than 3 mm predicts arrhythmia in TCA overdose with sensitivity around 80 percent; QRS over 160 ms predicts ventricular arrhythmia and indicates aggressive sodium bicarbonate dosing.

subtopic_detail: Distinguishing very late onset schizophrenia from delusional disorder in 72-year-old woman
stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatrist asked to review a 72-year-old woman with a six-month history of fixed persecutory belief that her neighbour is poisoning her water supply. She has installed cameras and tested her water. She has no hallucinations, no thought disorder, no negative symptoms, and remains otherwise able to manage her household and pay bills. There is no family history of schizophrenia, no recent drug use, normal MMSE, and unremarkable MRI brain.

data_table.Mental state: Calm, fixed non-bizarre delusion, no hallucinations, no thought disorder, mood euthymic, insight nil
data_table.Investigations: MMSE 28/30; MRI brain unremarkable; bloods normal; urine drug screen negative; TSH 2.4
data_table.Functional: Independent in activities of daily living, paying bills, shopping, driving

lead_in: What is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Very late onset schizophrenia-like psychosis with intact higher cognition and preserved function
options.rationale: Very late onset schizophrenia-like psychosis typically includes hallucinations or thought disorder; this is absent here. (Source: RANZCP; ICD-11)

options.text: Alzheimer disease with associated paranoid behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia first
options.rationale: Alzheimer disease usually shows cognitive impairment on MMSE and imaging changes; her cognition is preserved. (Source: RANZCP)

options.text: Substance-induced psychotic disorder from her recent over-the-counter and prescription medications
options.rationale: No substance use is identified and urine drug screen is negative; substance-induced disorder is not supported. (Source: RANZCP)

options.text: Delusional disorder persecutory type with circumscribed non-bizarre belief and preserved function
options.rationale: Circumscribed non-bizarre persecutory delusion with no hallucinations or thought disorder and preserved function defines delusional disorder. (Source: RANZCP; ICD-11)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder with mood instability and persistent delusion across affective phases now
options.rationale: Schizoaffective requires both affective and psychotic features with characteristic phasic pattern, which is absent. (Source: RANZCP)

explanation.summary: Delusional disorder is characterised by one or more non-bizarre delusions for at least one month with otherwise preserved function, no prominent hallucinations or thought disorder, and no significant negative symptoms. Persecutory type is the most common subtype in late life. Very late onset schizophrenia-like psychosis usually includes hallucinations or thought disorder. Cognitive testing and imaging help exclude dementia. Treatment is challenging because insight is typically nil; second-generation antipsychotics at low dose are first-line with strong attention to therapeutic alliance.
key_points: Non-bizarre fixed delusion with preserved function
No prominent hallucinations or thought disorder
Persecutory subtype most common in late life
Second-generation antipsychotic at low dose first-line
explanation.pearls: Risperidone 0.5 to 2 mg daily is the most commonly used first-line agent in late-life delusional disorder, but adherence is the rate-limiting step because insight is typically absent and the therapeutic alliance is fragile.

subtopic_detail: First-line imaging for suspected ureteric calculus in adult with single haematuria episode
stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 40-year-old man with acute left flank pain radiating to the groin, microscopic haematuria, and no fever. He has no prior stone history. His eGFR is 88 mL/min/1.73 m2 and bedside ultrasound shows mild left hydronephrosis. He is otherwise well, normotensive, and tolerating oral fluids.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 92, RR 18, BP 124/76, SpO2 99 percent
data_table.Examination: Left flank tenderness, no rebound, no palpable mass, no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis 2+ blood, no nitrites, no leucocytes; WCC 8.6; eGFR 88; bedside US mild left hydronephrosis

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line definitive imaging?

options.text: Plain KUB film alone, sufficient to confirm a radiopaque calculus and guide initial treatment now
options.rationale: Plain KUB misses about 30 percent of stones and underestimates obstruction; CT KUB has replaced it for first-line workup. (Source: USANZ; RANZCR)

options.text: Magnetic resonance urogram, providing accurate stone detection without any ionising radiation exposure
options.rationale: MR urogram has limited stone visualisation, restricted availability, and high cost; CT KUB is first-line. (Source: USANZ)

options.text: Intravenous urogram with contrast, providing functional and anatomical imaging in a single examination
options.rationale: IVU is largely superseded by CT KUB for stones because of higher contrast dose and lower sensitivity. (Source: USANZ)

options.text: Repeat formal radiology renal ultrasound with full bladder views and Doppler resistive index measurement
options.rationale: Ultrasound is useful in pregnancy and children but has lower sensitivity for ureteric stones in adults. (Source: USANZ)

options.text: Non-contrast low-dose CT KUB, the first-line investigation for adult suspected ureteric calculus disease
options.rationale: Non-contrast low-dose CT KUB is the first-line imaging in adults with suspected ureteric calculus with sensitivity over 95 percent. (Source: USANZ; RANZCR)

explanation.summary: Non-contrast low-dose CT KUB is the first-line investigation for adult suspected ureteric calculus, providing high sensitivity and specificity, stone characterisation, and assessment of complications. Ultrasound is the preferred first investigation in pregnancy and children to reduce radiation. Indications for urgent urology referral include obstructed infected system, single functioning kidney, bilateral obstruction, refractory pain or vomiting, and acute kidney injury. Stones less than 5 mm typically pass spontaneously.
key_points: CT KUB is first-line imaging in adults
Ultrasound preferred in pregnancy and paediatrics
Urology if obstructed-infected, AKI, solitary kidney, or refractory symptoms
Stones under 5 mm usually pass spontaneously
explanation.pearls: Low-dose CT KUB protocols deliver effective dose around 2 mSv compared with 8 to 10 mSv for standard CT, which has driven its adoption as first-line over IVU in the past decade.

subtopic_detail: First-line pharmacotherapy for newly diagnosed trigeminal neuralgia in 58-year-old woman
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 58-year-old woman with two months of brief electric shock pains in the right cheek triggered by chewing and brushing teeth. Episodes last seconds and resolve completely between attacks. She has no facial numbness, no weakness, and a normal neurological examination. She has no liver disease, normal renal function, and no allergy to anticonvulsants. MRI brain reported as no demyelination and a normal trigeminal nerve.

data_table.Examination: Trigger zone right cheek to light touch, no sensory deficit, normal corneal reflex, normal jaw jerk
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain: no demyelination, no vascular loop on trigeminal nerve; LFTs ALT 22; eGFR 90; Na 138
data_table.Past history: Nil regular medication; no Asian heritage HLA-B*1502 risk; no allergy

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Gabapentin 300 mg three times daily titrated up over two weeks against pain and sedation tolerance
options.rationale: Gabapentin is a second-line agent in trigeminal neuralgia; carbamazepine has the best evidence. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Amitriptyline 25 mg at night titrated upwards as needed for trigeminal neuralgia symptomatic relief
options.rationale: Amitriptyline is used for other neuropathic pain but is not first-line for trigeminal neuralgia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Carbamazepine 100 mg twice daily titrated up against symptoms with sodium and LFT monitoring
options.rationale: Carbamazepine is the first-line evidence-based agent for trigeminal neuralgia with sodium and LFT monitoring during titration. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Oral paracetamol modified release 1330 mg three times daily as the first-line analgesic regimen
options.rationale: Simple analgesics are ineffective in trigeminal neuralgia which is neuropathic; sodium channel blockers are required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Refer for microvascular decompression surgery before any pharmacological trial as definitive treatment
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for medication failure or intolerance; pharmacotherapy is first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

explanation.summary: Trigeminal neuralgia is characterised by brief electric shock pains in the trigeminal distribution triggered by light touch or chewing. Carbamazepine is the first-line evidence-based agent with response rates around 70 percent. Oxcarbazepine is an alternative with fewer drug interactions. Baclofen and lamotrigine are add-on options. Surgical options including microvascular decompression are reserved for medication failure or intolerance. Patients of Han Chinese, Thai, and other South Asian descent require HLA-B*1502 testing before carbamazepine due to risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
key_points: Carbamazepine first-line with 70 percent response
Monitor sodium and LFTs during titration
HLA-B*1502 testing in at-risk ancestry
Surgery reserved for medication failure
explanation.pearls: Carbamazepine response in trigeminal neuralgia is so reliable that lack of response within four weeks at adequate dose should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis or MRI for secondary causes such as a vascular loop or demyelination.

subtopic_detail: Diagnostic confirmation manoeuvre and treatment for posterior canal BPPV in 62-year-old
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 62-year-old man with three weeks of brief positional vertigo on rolling over in bed and looking up. Episodes last around 30 seconds, resolve completely, and are accompanied by nausea. He has no hearing loss, no tinnitus, no continuous unsteadiness, and no neurological deficit. His cervical spine has no rotational restriction and he has no significant carotid disease.

data_table.Examination: Cranial nerves normal, no nystagmus at rest, no cerebellar signs, normal gait, no lateralising neurological signs
data_table.Investigations: Routine bloods normal; no imaging performed; audiometry not yet done
data_table.Past history: Hypertension on ramipril; no recent head injury

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next combined diagnostic and therapeutic manoeuvre?

options.text: Order MRI internal auditory meatus to exclude vestibular schwannoma and review imaging report
options.rationale: MRI is reserved for atypical features, neurological signs, or hearing loss; this is classic posterior canal BPPV. (Source: RACGP: Vertigo)

options.text: Trial regular oral prochlorperazine 5 mg three times daily for two weeks for vestibular sedation
options.rationale: Vestibular suppressants are not curative in BPPV and prolong recovery; particle repositioning is first-line. (Source: RACGP: Vertigo)

options.text: Refer to ENT for diagnostic vestibular testing including videonystagmography and rotational chair tests
options.rationale: Specialist vestibular testing is reserved for atypical or refractory cases; bedside Dix-Hallpike with Epley is first-line. (Source: RACGP: Vertigo)

options.text: Perform Dix-Hallpike manoeuvre and if positive then apply Epley canalith repositioning manoeuvre
options.rationale: Dix-Hallpike confirms posterior canal BPPV and Epley repositioning is the first-line therapeutic manoeuvre with about 80 percent single-treatment success. (Source: RACGP: Vertigo)

options.text: Prescribe vestibular rehabilitation home exercise program alone and review four weeks later in clinic
options.rationale: Vestibular rehabilitation alone is slower than particle repositioning; Epley is the proven first-line treatment. (Source: RACGP: Vertigo)

explanation.summary: Posterior canal benign paroxysmal positional vertigo is diagnosed by the Dix-Hallpike manoeuvre eliciting torsional upbeat nystagmus that latency, fatigues, and resolves. The Epley canalith repositioning manoeuvre achieves resolution in about 80 percent after one treatment and over 90 percent after two. Vestibular suppressants prolong recovery and are not first-line. MRI is reserved for atypical features such as central nystagmus, focal neurological signs, hearing loss, or persistent symptoms. Recurrence is common and patients should be taught self-treatment.
key_points: Dix-Hallpike confirms posterior canal BPPV
Epley achieves about 80 percent single-treatment resolution
Vestibular suppressants prolong recovery
MRI for atypical features or persistent symptoms
explanation.pearls: Posterior canal BPPV nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike has a 5 to 20 second latency, lasts under 60 seconds, and fatigues with repetition; absence of these features should prompt evaluation for central causes.

subtopic_detail: Saddle anaesthesia and urinary retention with new lower back pain requires urgent imaging
stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 48-year-old man with three days of severe lower back pain after lifting at work. He now describes new perineal numbness, difficulty initiating urination, and reduced erection. Bladder scan shows 700 mL post-void residual. Examination confirms saddle anaesthesia, reduced anal tone, and bilateral leg weakness. Reflexes are reduced at the ankles. He has no fever and no systemic illness.

data_table.Examination: Saddle anaesthesia, reduced anal tone, bilateral leg weakness power 4/5, absent ankle jerks, normal plantars
data_table.Investigations: Bladder scan 700 mL post-void residual; WCC 9.2; CRP 6; no fever
data_table.Setting: Tertiary hospital with MRI 24/7 available; spinal team on call

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Outpatient lumbar spine X-ray with flexion and extension views to assess instability and review in clinic
options.rationale: Plain films do not show neural compression; MRI is required for suspected cauda equina syndrome. (Source: RACS; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Lumbar puncture with measurement of opening pressure and cell count and biochemistry analysis
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated when cord or cauda equina compression is suspected before imaging. (Source: RACS)

options.text: Outpatient CT lumbar spine within 48 hours, scheduled through GP referral pathway after discharge
options.rationale: CT has lower soft tissue and neural resolution than MRI and 48 hour delay risks irreversible deficit. (Source: RACS)

options.text: Outpatient nerve conduction studies in two weeks to assess for peripheral neuropathy or radiculopathy
options.rationale: Nerve conduction studies do not show structural compression and a two-week delay risks permanent loss. (Source: RACS)

options.text: Urgent whole lumbosacral spine MRI within hours to confirm cauda equina compression for surgery
options.rationale: Whole lumbosacral spine MRI within hours is the standard investigation for suspected cauda equina syndrome to confirm compression and guide urgent surgery. (Source: RACS)

explanation.summary: Cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency caused by compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots, most commonly from a central disc protrusion. Red flag features include saddle anaesthesia, bladder or bowel dysfunction with post-void residual over 500 mL, sexual dysfunction, and bilateral leg weakness. Whole lumbosacral spine MRI within hours confirms the diagnosis and surgical decompression within 48 hours of symptom onset preserves the best functional outcome. Delayed diagnosis risks permanent bladder, bowel, and sexual dysfunction.
key_points: Saddle anaesthesia and retention with PVR over 500 mL are red flags
Urgent MRI within hours
Decompression ideally within 48 hours of symptom onset
Plain films and outpatient pathways are inappropriate
explanation.pearls: Bladder PVR over 500 mL with saddle anaesthesia carries a positive predictive value of about 50 percent for cauda equina on MRI, which is why the imaging threshold is low when both features coexist.

subtopic_detail: Single attack of monocular vision loss with pain on eye movement and normal fundoscopy
stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 28-year-old woman with two days of progressive blurred vision in the right eye with pain on eye movement. Visual acuity is 6/24 right and 6/6 left with a right relative afferent pupillary defect. Fundoscopy is normal in both eyes. There is no headache, no fever, and no systemic symptoms. She has no prior neurological episodes and is otherwise well.

data_table.Examination: Right RAPD, reduced colour vision right, normal fundoscopy bilaterally, normal cranial nerves otherwise, no cerebellar or long tract signs
data_table.Investigations: Routine bloods unremarkable; OCT not yet performed
data_table.Past history: Nil regular medication; no prior optic or neurological event

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Visual field testing with Humphrey perimetry in outpatient clinic in two weeks alongside OCT
options.rationale: Outpatient fields delay assessment of demyelinating risk; urgent MRI brain and orbits is the priority. (Source: MSWA; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Urgent CT brain with contrast to exclude intracranial mass lesion as the diagnostic next step
options.rationale: CT brain has low yield for demyelination; MRI brain and orbits is the standard investigation. (Source: MSWA)

options.text: MRI brain and orbits with contrast to assess for demyelinating lesions and confirm optic neuritis
options.rationale: MRI brain and orbits with contrast is the recommended investigation to confirm optic neuritis and assess demyelinating disease risk. (Source: MSWA)

options.text: Routine outpatient ophthalmology review in six weeks with no imaging pending symptom progression
options.rationale: Delayed review risks missing demyelinating disease that benefits from early disease-modifying therapy. (Source: MSWA)

options.text: Empiric oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 14 days as immediate treatment without imaging input
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone monotherapy at conventional doses worsens outcomes in optic neuritis; high-dose IV methylprednisolone is used when treatment is indicated. (Source: MSWA)

explanation.summary: Optic neuritis presents with subacute monocular vision loss, pain on eye movement, dyschromatopsia, and a relative afferent pupillary defect. Fundoscopy is normal in retrobulbar optic neuritis. MRI brain and orbits with contrast confirms inflammation and stratifies multiple sclerosis risk: T2 white matter lesions raise 10-year MS risk to over 70 percent. Intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days speeds recovery but does not improve final acuity. Oral prednisolone at conventional doses worsens outcomes and should be avoided as monotherapy.
key_points: Subacute monocular vision loss with eye movement pain
RAPD plus dyschromatopsia with normal fundoscopy
MRI brain and orbits with contrast
Avoid oral prednisolone monotherapy
explanation.pearls: Among patients with optic neuritis and at least one T2 brain lesion on baseline MRI, the 10-year cumulative risk of multiple sclerosis is around 72 percent versus 25 percent with a normal MRI, which underpins the urgency of imaging for prognostic and treatment decisions.

subtopic_detail: Pharmacological management of acute idiopathic facial nerve palsy at 36 hours of onset
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 36-year-old man with 36 hours of right-sided facial weakness affecting forehead, eyelid, and corner of mouth. He cannot fully close the right eye and has subjective hyperacusis. There is no vesicular rash on the ear or palate, no hearing loss, and no other neurological deficit. He is otherwise well, has no diabetes, no immunosuppression, and is not pregnant.

data_table.Examination: House-Brackmann grade IV right LMN facial palsy, intact corneal reflex, no vesicles, normal otoscopy bilaterally, normal taste anteriorly
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 5.8 mmol/L; HbA1c not yet done; no further imaging
data_table.Past history: Nil regular medication; no recent zoster or VZV exposure

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacological management?

options.text: Topical aciclovir cream to the affected side three times daily for two weeks as antiviral monotherapy
options.rationale: Topical aciclovir is not effective in Bell palsy; oral prednisolone is the proven intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Oral valaciclovir 1 g three times daily for one week as antiviral monotherapy and outpatient review
options.rationale: Antiviral monotherapy without corticosteroid does not improve recovery rates in Bell palsy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for three days inpatient with cardiac monitoring after admission
options.rationale: IV pulse steroid is not standard for Bell palsy; oral prednisolone within 72 hours is the proven intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 50 mg daily for 10 days commenced within 72 hours of symptom onset with eye care
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone 50 mg daily for 10 days started within 72 hours improves recovery rates in Bell palsy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Combined oral prednisolone 50 mg daily plus oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily for 10 days now
options.rationale: Adding antiviral to prednisolone does not improve outcomes in classic Bell palsy without vesicles. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Idiopathic facial nerve palsy is the most common cause of acute lower motor neuron facial weakness. Oral prednisolone 50 mg daily for 10 days started within 72 hours of onset improves recovery rates and is the only intervention with reliable benefit. Antivirals add no clear benefit in classic Bell palsy without vesicles but should be added if Ramsay Hunt syndrome is suspected. Eye care with lubricant drops, ointment, and taping during sleep is essential to prevent exposure keratitis. Improvement typically begins by three weeks and most patients recover by six months.
key_points: Oral prednisolone 50 mg daily for 10 days within 72 hours
Antivirals not routinely added in classic Bell palsy
Add antivirals if Ramsay Hunt vesicles present
Eye care critical to prevent exposure keratitis
explanation.pearls: Adding aciclovir to prednisolone does not improve House-Brackmann grade I or II recovery at 9 months in classic Bell palsy without vesicles, which is why monotherapy with prednisolone is preferred in Australian practice.

subtopic_detail: Choice of biologic for severe eosinophilic asthma uncontrolled on triple inhaled therapy in adult
stem: You are the respiratory registrar reviewing a 42-year-old non-smoking woman with severe asthma referred from her GP. She remains symptomatic with two hospitalisations in 12 months despite high-dose ICS-LABA plus tiotropium and good adherence. Blood eosinophils are 540 cells/microL, FeNO 65 ppb, total IgE 110 IU/mL, and she has nasal polyps. No specific aeroallergen sensitisation is documented. She has no parasitic infection risk and is not pregnant.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 80, RR 18, BP 124/78, SpO2 98 percent
data_table.Examination: Mild end-expiratory wheeze, nasal polyps on rhinoscopy, no clubbing
data_table.Investigations: Blood eosinophils 540 cells/microL; FeNO 65 ppb; total IgE 110 IU/mL; spirometry FEV1 62 percent predicted; specific IgE panel negative for perennial allergens
data_table.Medications: ICS-LABA 1000 mcg/250 mcg twice daily, tiotropium 5 mcg daily, montelukast 10 mg daily

lead_in: Which biologic is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Omalizumab subcutaneous monthly dosed by weight and total IgE level as anti-IgE biologic first choice
options.rationale: Omalizumab requires documented sensitisation to a perennial aeroallergen, which is absent in this patient. (Source: TSANZ; PBS criteria)

options.text: Mepolizumab subcutaneous 100 mg every four weeks as IL-5 biologic monotherapy ongoing
options.rationale: Mepolizumab is reasonable but does not address her nasal polyps as effectively as dupilumab in this phenotype. (Source: TSANZ; PBS criteria)

options.text: Reslizumab IV monthly weight-based dosing for severe eosinophilic asthma as the next step now
options.rationale: Reslizumab is IV monthly and reserved when subcutaneous options are unsuitable; not first preference here. (Source: TSANZ; PBS criteria)

options.text: Benralizumab subcutaneous every eight weeks after loading as IL-5 receptor antibody monotherapy
options.rationale: Benralizumab is an option for eosinophilic asthma but does not target the type 2 polyp pathway as broadly as dupilumab. (Source: TSANZ; PBS criteria)

options.text: Dupilumab subcutaneous every two weeks for severe type 2 asthma with nasal polyps as combined indication
options.rationale: Dupilumab targets IL-4 and IL-13 and treats type 2 asthma with nasal polyps as a combined indication on Australian PBS criteria. (Source: TSANZ; PBS criteria)

explanation.summary: Severe asthma not controlled on triple inhaled therapy is eligible for biologic therapy. Choice depends on phenotype: omalizumab requires perennial allergen sensitisation, mepolizumab and benralizumab require elevated eosinophil counts, and dupilumab targets type 2 inflammation including asthma with nasal polyps or atopic dermatitis. Tezepelumab is approved for severe asthma irrespective of phenotype. The Australian Severe Asthma Toolkit and PBS criteria define eligibility. Switching between biologics is reasonable if response is inadequate after four to six months.
key_points: Omalizumab requires perennial allergen sensitisation
Mepolizumab, benralizumab for eosinophilic phenotype
Dupilumab for type 2 asthma plus nasal polyps
Tezepelumab is phenotype-independent
explanation.pearls: Dupilumab reduces nasal polyp size and improves asthma exacerbations by around 50 percent in patients with both conditions and is the only biologic with dual PBS indication for adult asthma plus chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps.

subtopic_detail: Distinguishing isolated polymyalgia rheumatica from giant cell arteritis overlap on presentation
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 72-year-old woman with three weeks of bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain with morning stiffness over an hour. She also describes a new bitemporal headache that has worsened over five days, jaw claudication after eating tough foods, and a single brief episode of right monocular blurred vision two days ago. There is no rash, no fever, and no joint swelling. ESR is 102 and CRP is 88.

data_table.Examination: Limited shoulder and hip range with pain, no synovitis, mild bitemporal scalp tenderness, no visual deficit on examination, palpable temporal arteries with reduced pulsation right
data_table.Investigations: ESR 102 mm/hr; CRP 88 mg/L; Hb 112; platelets 410; LFTs ALP 180 with normal bilirubin; eGFR 78
data_table.Past history: Nil regular medication; no recent infection

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral prednisolone 15 mg daily for PMR symptoms with rapid taper over six weeks as monotherapy outpatient
options.rationale: The cranial features and jaw claudication indicate GCA overlap requiring high dose prednisolone, not PMR dose. (Source: ARA; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Oral NSAID such as celecoxib 200 mg twice daily and book temporal artery biopsy in two weeks for diagnosis
options.rationale: NSAIDs do not treat GCA and biopsy must not delay steroid initiation when visual symptoms are present. (Source: ARA)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily and arrange temporal artery biopsy within 14 days of treatment start
options.rationale: Visual symptoms and jaw claudication mandate immediate high dose prednisolone with biopsy within 14 days for confirmation. (Source: ARA; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange outpatient rheumatology referral within four weeks without initiating any steroid before assessment
options.rationale: Delay risks permanent visual loss; steroids start before specialist review when GCA is suspected. (Source: ARA)

options.text: Intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for three days for monocular visual loss already established prior
options.rationale: IV pulse is reserved for confirmed or progressing visual loss; transient amaurosis without current deficit is treated with high dose oral prednisolone. (Source: ARA)

explanation.summary: Up to 50 percent of patients with giant cell arteritis have polymyalgia rheumatica features and 15 to 20 percent of PMR patients develop GCA. Red flags for GCA overlap include new bitemporal headache, jaw claudication, transient or persistent monocular vision loss, scalp tenderness, and constitutional symptoms. Suspected GCA mandates immediate oral prednisolone 40 to 60 mg daily, or IV pulse for established visual loss, with temporal artery biopsy within 14 days. Methotrexate and tocilizumab are steroid-sparing options. Untreated GCA risks irreversible blindness.
key_points: Jaw claudication and amaurosis are red flags for GCA
Start oral prednisolone immediately, do not delay for biopsy
Biopsy within 14 days of steroid initiation
IV pulse for persistent or progressing visual loss
explanation.pearls: Temporal artery biopsy sensitivity falls from about 80 to 60 percent if delayed beyond 14 days of steroid treatment, which is why the biopsy window is tight even though steroids start immediately.
